title: Assessment: Use Mocks, Fakes, and Contracts Intentionally

questions.question: What makes a mock 'strict' in the sense taught by this course?

options.A: It records every possible call forever
options.B: It is constrained to the real interface and used at a narrow owned collaborator boundary
options.C: It replaces every dependency in the module

questions.explanation: Strictness comes from autospec-like constraints and narrow use at collaborators the application owns.

questions.question: Where should a Python patch usually be applied?

options.A: Where the dependency was first implemented
options.B: Where the code under test looks it up

questions.explanation: Patching the lookup site changes the object the code under test actually uses.

questions.question: When is a verified fake often better than a deep mock?

options.A: When you need speed plus a stronger connection to real boundary behavior
options.B: When you want to avoid learning the external system at all

questions.explanation: Verified fakes keep tests fast but are anchored more closely to real service behavior than deep mocks are.

questions.question: Explain what question the course uses to choose among mocks, fakes, contract checks, and integration tests.

required_points: Choose the cheapest test that still tells the truth about the boundary
Owned collaborators often justify strict mocks
External boundaries often need higher-fidelity checks
answer_key.full_credit: Explains the decision rule and distinguishes owned-collaborator use from external-boundary use
answer_key.partial_credit: Mentions truth or cost but not both

questions.prompt: A team is deeply mocking a payment SDK and getting brittle tests. Use Module 5 to argue for a better combination of seam design, strict mocking, and higher-fidelity alternatives where appropriate.
questions.guidance: Strong answers narrow mocks to owned collaborators, move provider detail behind an adapter, and justify when verified fakes, contracts, or integration checks should replace deep mocks.

rubric.A: Presents a clear migration away from deep external mocks with defensible replacement choices
rubric.B: Suggests fewer mocks but leaves the alternative coverage model unclear
rubric.C: Mostly argues from preference without boundary-specific reasoning
